Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Multilingual_support_(Indic) "Help:Multilingual support (Indic)"), you may see boxes or letters that did not properly join into syllables instead of Indic text.

(डोगरी, 𑠖𑠵𑠌𑠤𑠮, ڈوگری, *Ḍōgrī*, [[ɖoːɡ.ɾiː]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")) is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_language "Indo-Aryan language") of the [Western Pahari](/wiki/Western_Pahari "Western Pahari") group,[[5]](#cite_note-book004-5) primarily spoken by the [Dogra people](/wiki/Dogra_people "Dogra people") native to the [Duggar Region](/wiki/Duggar_Region "Duggar Region") of [Jammu Division](/wiki/Jammu_Division "Jammu Division") of Indian-administered [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)"); with smaller groups of speakers in the adjoining regions of the Indian states of [Himachal Pradesh](/wiki/Himachal_Pradesh "Himachal Pradesh") and [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) as well as Pakistan-administered [Azad Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir") and the Pakistani province of [Punjab](/wiki/Pakistan_Punjab "Pakistan Punjab").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Indo-Aryan language spoken primarily in Jammu

Not to be confused with the [Dogrib language](/wiki/Dogrib_language "Dogrib language") of Canada.

**Dogri** (डोगरी, 𑠖𑠵𑠌𑠤𑠮, ڈوگری, *Ḍōgrī*, [[ɖoːɡ.ɾiː]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")) is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_language "Indo-Aryan language") of the [Western Pahari](/wiki/Western_Pahari "Western Pahari") group,[[5]](#cite_note-book004-5) primarily spoken by the [Dogra people](/wiki/Dogra_people "Dogra people") native to the [Duggar Region](/wiki/Duggar_Region "Duggar Region") of [Jammu Division](/wiki/Jammu_Division "Jammu Division") of Indian-administered [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)"); with smaller groups of speakers in the adjoining regions of the Indian states of [Himachal Pradesh](/wiki/Himachal_Pradesh "Himachal Pradesh") and [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) as well as Pakistan-administered [Azad Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir") and the Pakistani province of [Punjab](/wiki/Pakistan_Punjab "Pakistan Punjab").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

It is currently spoken in the districts of [Kathua](/wiki/Kathua_district "Kathua district"), [Jammu](/wiki/Jammu_district "Jammu district"), [Samba](/wiki/Samba_district "Samba district"), [Udhampur](/wiki/Udhampur_district "Udhampur district") and parts of [Reasi District](/wiki/Reasi_District "Reasi District") (mostly in Reasi, Katra and Pouni Tehsil) of [Jammu division](/wiki/Jammu_division "Jammu division").[[3]](#cite_note-census2011-3) Unusually for an [Indo-European language](/wiki/Indo-European_language "Indo-European language"), Dogri is [tonal](/wiki/Tonal_language "Tonal language"),[[8]](#cite_note-book005-8) a trait it shares with other Western Pahari languages and [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"). It has several varieties, all with greater than 80% lexical similarity.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Dogri is spoken by 2.6 million people in India (as of the 2011 census).[[3]](#cite_note-census2011-3) It has been among the country's 22 [scheduled languages](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India") since 2003. It is also one of the five official languages of the union territory of Jammu and Kashmir.

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg/40px-Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg)

For a list of words relating to Dogri language, see the [***Dogri language*** category of words](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Category:Dogri_language "wiktionary:Category:Dogri language") in [Wiktionary](/wiki/Wiktionary "Wiktionary"), the free dictionary.

## Script

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dogri_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Script")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Dogra.png/250px-Dogra.png)](/wiki/File:Dogra.png)

Dogra Script Specimen

Dogri was originally written in the old Dogra Akkhar script – a modified version of [Takri](/wiki/Takri_script "Takri script").[[10]](#cite_note-10) A modified version of this script was created by the order of [Maharaja Ranbir Singh](/wiki/Ranbir_Singh_of_Jammu_and_Kashmir "Ranbir Singh of Jammu and Kashmir") of Jammu and Kashmir, which was then called [Name Dogra Akkhar](/wiki/Name_Dogra_Akkhar "Name Dogra Akkhar").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Official documents were written in this new script; however it never caught on among the general Dogri-speaking populace. Currently, [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") is the officially recognised script for Dogri in India and virtually all Dogri literature is published in it.

* Gemination occurs in all consonants except the consonants /ɾ ɳ ɽ ʃ/.
* Retroflex consonants /ɽ ɳ/ rarely occur in word initial position.
* /f z x ɣ/ only occur from Perso-Arabic loan words. /f/ is also heard as an allophone of /pʰ/.
* /ɾ/ can also marginally be heard as trilled [r] in some speech.
* In some words, /s/ can become more weakly pronounced, or even eliminated and replaced by a glottal fricative sound [h].
* A palatal nasal sound [ɲ] typically occurs when a dental nasal precedes a post-alveolar affricate consonant, rarely occurring in words word-initially or medially.
* A velar nasal sound [ŋ] typically occurs when a dental nasal precedes a velar plosive consonant, and rarely occurs word-initially or medially.[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12)

## Tone rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dogri_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Tone rules")]

These are rules of writing tones in Dogri using [Devanagari Script](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari"). They are as follows:-

* Just like [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), Dogri also uses the letters घ (gʱə), झ (d͡ʒʱə), ढ (ɖʱə), ध (d̪ʱə), भ (bʱə) and ढ़ (ɽʱə) for tonal uses. When at the beginning of the word, it has a high-falling tone; i.e.:- घ (kə̂), झ (t͡ʃə̂), ढ (ʈə̂), ध (t̪ə̂), भ (pə̂) and ढ़ (ɽə̂). When in the middle and final position of the word, the preceding vowel has a low-rising tone; i.e.:- अघ (ə̌ɡ), अझ (ə̌d͡ʒ), अढ (ə̌ɖ), अध (ə̌d̪), अभ (ə̌b) and अढ़ (ə̌ɽ). Examples:- घड़ी (kə̂ɽiː)- clock, and बध (bə̌d̪).
* Unlike [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), there is no ह (ɦə) sound and it has a high-falling tone in all positions; i.e.:- हत्थ (ə̂t̪ːʰə)- hand.
* To indicate a low-rising tone in the middle of words, Dogri uses ह् (ह with a [halant](/wiki/Virama "Virama")) to indicate it when the preceding vowel is long; i.e.:- आ (aː), ई (iː), ऊ (uː), ए (eː), ऐ (ɛː), ओ (oː) and औ (ɔː). Example:- साह्ब (sǎːb)- [sahab](/wiki/Sahib "Sahib"). When the preceding vowel is short, i.e., - अ (ə), इ (ɪ) and उ (ʊ); a [combining apostrophe](/wiki/Modifier_letter_apostrophe "Modifier letter apostrophe") (ʼ) is used. Example:- लʼत्त (lə̌tː)- leg.
* The characters mentioned in the first point can also be used to indicate high-falling tone in the middle of the words when between a short vowel and a long vowel.

## Historical references

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dogri_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Historical references")]

In the year 1317, [Amir Khusro](/wiki/Amir_Khusro "Amir Khusro"), the famous [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") and [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") poet, referred to Duger (Dogri) while describing the languages and dialects of India as follows: *"Sindhi-o-Lahori-o-Kashmiri-o-Duger."*[[14]](#cite_note-book0a2-14)[[15]](#cite_note-book0a3-15)

## Official status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dogri_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Official status")]

Dogri is one of the 22 [Scheduled languages of India](/wiki/Scheduled_languages_of_India "Scheduled languages of India"), officially recognized in December 2003 through the 92nd Constitutional Amendment, which added it to the Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution. In September 2020, the Parliament of India passed the Jammu and Kashmir Official Languages Act, declaring Dogri as one of the five official languages of the Union Territory of Jammu and Kashmir, along with Hindi, Urdu, English, and Kashmiri.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Theories on name origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dogri_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Theories on name origin")]

Intellectuals in the court of Maharaja [Ranbir Singh](/wiki/Ranbir_Singh_of_Jammu_and_Kashmir "Ranbir Singh of Jammu and Kashmir") of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(princely_state) "Jammu and Kashmir (princely state)") described *[Duggar](/wiki/Duggar_(region) "Duggar (region)")* as a distorted form of the word *Dwigarta*, which means 'two troughs', a possible reference to the [Mansar](/wiki/Mansar_Lake "Mansar Lake") and [Surinsar](/wiki/Surinsar_Lake "Surinsar Lake") lakes.[[17]](#cite_note-book0b1-17)

The linguist [George Grierson](/wiki/George_Abraham_Grierson "George Abraham Grierson") connected the term *Duggar* with the [Rajasthani](/wiki/Rajasthani_language "Rajasthani language") word *Doonger* which means 'hill', and *Dogra* with *Donger*.[[17]](#cite_note-book0b1-17) This opinion has lacked support because of the inconsistency of the ostensible changes from Rajasthani to Dogri (essentially the question of how *Doonger* became *Duggar* while *Donger* became *Dogra*), and has been contradicted by some scholars.[[18]](#cite_note-book0b2-18)

Yet another proposal stems from the word *Durgara*, the name of a kingdom mentioned in an eleventh century copper-plate inscription in the Bhuri Singh Museum in Chamba, Himachal Pradesh.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The word *Durgara* means 'invincible' in several Northern Indo-Aryan languages, and could be an allusion to the ruggedness of the terrain of Duggar and the historically militarised and autonomous Dogra societies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1976, the experts attending the Language Session of the All India Oriental Conference held in Dharwad, [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), could not reach consensus on the *Dwigarta* and *Durgara* hypotheses, but did manage agreement on a *Doonger*-*Duggar* connection. In a subsequent All India Oriental Conference held at [Jaipur](/wiki/Jaipur "Jaipur") in 1982, the linguists agreed that the culture, language and history of [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan") and Duggar share some similarities. It was also suggested that the words *Duggar* and *Dogra* are common in some parts of Rajasthan. Specifically, it was asserted that areas with many forts are called Duggar, and their inhabitants are accordingly known as Dogras. The land of Duggar also has many forts, which may support the above opinion.

An article by Dharam Chand Prashant in the literary magazine *Shiraza Dogri* suggested that "the opinion that the word *Duggar* is a form of the word *Duggarh* sounds appropriate."[[19]](#cite_note-book0b3-19)

## Recent history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dogri_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Recent history")]

Dogri folk song sung during the interview of a Dhramshala citizen

In modern times, a notable Dogri translation (in the New Dogra script) of the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") classic mathematical opus *[Lilavati](/wiki/Lilavati "Lilavati")*, by the noted mathematician [Bhaskaracharya](/wiki/Bhaskara_II "Bhaskara II") (b. 1114 AD), was published by the Vidya Vilas Press, Jammu in 1873.[[20]](#cite_note-book0c0-20) As Sanskrit literacy remained confined to a few, the late Maharaja Ranbir Singh had the *Lilavati* translated into Dogri by Jyotshi Bisheshwar, then principal of Jammu Pathshala.[[21]](#cite_note-book0c01-21)

Dogri has an established tradition of poetry, fiction and dramatic works. Recent poets range from the 18th-century Dogri poet Kavi Dattu (1725–1780) in Raja Ranjit Dev's court to Professor [Ram Nath Shastri](/wiki/Ram_Nath_Shastri "Ram Nath Shastri") and Mrs. [Padma Sachdev](/wiki/Padma_Sachdev "Padma Sachdev"). Kavi Dattu is highly regarded for his *Barah Massa* (Twelve Months), *Kamal Netra* (Lotus Eyes), *Bhup Bijog* and *Bir Bilas*.[[22]](#cite_note-book0c02-22) *[Shiraza Dogri](/wiki/Shiraza_Dogri "Shiraza Dogri")* is a Dogri literary periodical issued by the Jammu and Kashmir Academy of Art, Culture and Languages, which is a notable publisher of modern Dogri literary work, another being the Dogri Sanstha. Popular recent songs include *Pala Shapaiya Dogariya*, *Manne di Mauj* and *Shhori Deya*. The noted Pakistani singer [Malika Pukhraj](/wiki/Malika_Pukhraj "Malika Pukhraj") had roots in Duggar,[[23]](#cite_note-book0c03-23) and her renditions of several Dogri songs continue to be popular in the region. Some devotional songs ([bhajans](/wiki/Bhajan "Bhajan")) composed by [Karan Singh](/wiki/Karan_Singh "Karan Singh") have gained increasing popularity over time, including *Kaun Kareyaan Teri Aarti*.

Dogri programming features regularly on [Radio Kashmir](/wiki/Radio_Kashmir "Radio Kashmir") (a division of [All India Radio](/wiki/All_India_Radio "All India Radio")), and [Doordarshan](/wiki/Doordarshan "Doordarshan") (Indian state television) broadcasts in [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)"). However, Dogri does not have a dedicated state television channel yet, unlike Kashmiri (which has the Doordarshan Koshur channel, available on cable and satellite television throughout India).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Official recognition of the language has been gradual, but progressive. On 2 August 1969, the General Council of the [Sahitya Academy](/wiki/Sahitya_Academy "Sahitya Academy"), Delhi recognized Dogri as an "independent modern literary language" of India, based on the unanimous recommendation of a panel of linguists.[[24]](#cite_note-book0c1-24) Dogri is one of the official languages of the Indian union territory of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)"). On 22 December 2003, in a major milestone for the official status of the language, Dogri was recognized as a national language of India in the [Indian constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India").[[25]](#cite_note-book0c20-25)[[26]](#cite_note-book0c2-26)

In 2005, a collection of over 100 works of prose and poetry in Dogri published over the last 50 years was made accessible online at the [Central Institute of Indian Languages](/wiki/Central_Institute_of_Indian_Languages "Central Institute of Indian Languages") (CIIL), Mysore. This included works of eminent writer Dhinu Bhai Panth, Professor Madan Mohan Sharma, B.P. Sathai and Ram Nath Shastri.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
